questions.subject: Economy
questions.prompt: The India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA) is considered a landmark in India's trade policy primarily because:

options.text: It is the first agreement where India has granted 'Data Exclusivity' to European pharmaceutical companies.
It links market access concessions to a binding target of $100 billion in foreign direct investment and job creation.
It establishes a common currency for trade settlements between India and the four EFTA nations.
It is the first time India has signed a trade deal with a bloc that includes only landlocked countries.

questions.explanation: The defining feature of the India-EFTA TEPA is the 'binding commitment' regarding investment. Unlike traditional FTAs that focus primarily on tariff reductions, TEPA includes a legal commitment for EFTA nations to invest $100 billion into India and create 1 million jobs over 15 years. Option D is incorrect because Norway and Iceland are not landlocked.
questions.takeaway: TEPA introduces a new model for FTAs by tying trade benefits to tangible investment and employment outcomes.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: It is India's first FTA with a binding commitment linked to investment ($100 billion) and job creation (1 million direct jobs in 15 years).
